Izabel, the client has gone quiet. Two weeks of silence after that strong meeting. What do you make of it?

Let us not jump to conclusions. They replied warmly before, so they must still be interested. Their procurement lead was traveling, the silence could just be a scheduling thing. That is the most plausible read.
There is one red flag, though. A colleague saw their team at a supplier fair last week.
Then they might be comparing prices. But they cannot have signed with anyone yet, our quote is still open and they asked us to hold it. So I would rule out the worst case.
Fair. And the rival that suddenly cut its price yesterday, how do you read that?
That price is below cost, so they cannot be making money on it. My assumption is that they must have lost a big account and this is a defensive move. I would not speculate beyond that.
So what is your read overall? Should we worry?

My gut feeling is that they are stalling for leverage. If I read between the lines of that last email, the tone was warm. The idea that we are losing them is far-fetched. We should not hold back, I will call them Thursday.
They replied warmly, so they must still be interested.
It might be a scheduling delay, or it could be internal approvals.
Our quote is open, so they cannot have signed yet.
The price is below cost, so they must have lost an account.
Let us not jump to conclusions, but I would rule out the worst case.

Hi Izabel, it is Ethan, your account manager, thinking out loud about the client. Two weeks of silence after such a strong meeting. Honestly, I am trying not to jump to conclusions, but let me read the signals with you. First, they must be busy, not gone. Their procurement lead was traveling, so the delay could just be a scheduling thing. That is the plausible read. But there is one red flag. A colleague of mine saw their team at a supplier fair last week, so they might be talking to a rival to compare prices. Now, they cannot have signed with anyone yet, because our quote is still open and they asked us to hold it. My gut feeling? They are stalling to gain a little leverage. If I read between the lines of that last email, the tone was warmer than the silence suggests. So I would not speculate too far. I would rule out the worst case and simply call them Thursday. They must want the deal. They just want it on better terms. Talk soon.

Good morning, Izabel. It is Laura, your market analyst, with a quick read on the competitor. That sudden price cut yesterday surprised everyone, so let me help you interpret it rather than react. First, they cannot be making money at that number. It is below cost, which means the cut must be a short-term move to win one big account, not a new strategy. My assumption is simple. They must have lost a major client last quarter, and this looks like a defensive play. It might be a signal that their pipeline is thin. Now, some people will say we should match them, but I would rule that out. If we cut too, our margin would vanish, and honestly that could be exactly what they want. Do not read too much into one price, and do not hold back your own story. Your quality and lead time still win the serious buyers. My gut feeling is that this cut is far-fetched as a long-term threat. Read the signal for what it is, a bluff, and stay the course. Call me if you want to talk it through.
